questions.stem: Which system of differential equations is the SIR model without vital dynamics?
options: $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = \beta S I,\ \dfrac{dI}{dt} = \gamma I - \beta S I,\ \dfrac{dR}{dt} = -\gamma I$
$\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -\beta S I,\ \dfrac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I - \gamma I,\ \dfrac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I$
questions.explanation: Susceptibles fall through infection at rate $\beta S I$, infectives gain that same flow and lose recoveries at rate $\gamma I$, and recovered individuals accumulate at $\gamma I$. This gives $\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta S I$, $\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I - \gamma I$, $\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I$. The other systems have wrong signs, drop the bilinear $S I$ contact term, or remove the dependence on $I$ that the law of mass action requires.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: What are the standard initial conditions for the SIR model without vital dynamics at the start of an epidemic?
questions.explanation: At the onset of an epidemic almost everyone is susceptible, a small positive number is infective, and no one has yet recovered: $S(0) = S_0$, $I(0) = I_0 > 0$, $R(0) = 0$. Starting with no susceptibles or with a full recovered class would not represent the beginning of an outbreak, and the population must satisfy $S_0 + I_0 + R_0 = N$, not have every class equal to $N$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Setting $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = 0$ in the SIR model (with $I \neq 0$) shows that the infective curve peaks when $S$ equals
questions.explanation: From $\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I - \gamma I = 0$ with $I \neq 0$, divide by $I$ to get $\beta S = \gamma$, so $S = \gamma/\beta$. This is the susceptible level at which the infective population stops growing and turns over. The reciprocal $\beta/\gamma$ and the product $\beta\gamma$ do not satisfy the balance, and $N$ is the whole population, not the turning point.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: In the SIR analysis the relative removal rate is defined as $\rho = \dfrac{\gamma}{\beta}$. The infective population grows only while $S$ exceeds
questions.explanation: Since $\frac{dI}{dt} = (\beta S - \gamma) I$, the infectives increase exactly when $\beta S - \gamma > 0$, that is $S > \gamma/\beta = \rho$. So the threshold susceptible level is the relative removal rate $\rho$ itself. When $S$ drops below $\rho$ the epidemic begins to decline. The other expressions do not match the condition $\beta S > \gamma$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Dividing the infective equation by the susceptible equation in the SIR model gives $\dfrac{dI}{dS}$ equal to
questions.explanation: Form $\frac{dI}{dS} = \frac{\beta S I - \gamma I}{-\beta S I} = -1 + \frac{\gamma}{\beta S}$. Writing $\rho = \gamma/\beta$ gives $\frac{dI}{dS} = -1 + \frac{\rho}{S}$. The sign on the leading $-1$ comes from dividing by the negative $\frac{dS}{dt}$, and the second term carries $\rho$ over $S$. The other forms have a wrong sign or drop the $-1$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Integrating $\dfrac{dI}{dS} = -1 + \dfrac{\rho}{S}$ and applying the initial data yields the orbit equation
questions.explanation: Integrating gives $I = -S + \rho \ln|S| + c$. Using $I(0) = I_0$ and $S(0) = S_0$ fixes $c = I_0 + S_0 - \rho \ln S_0$, so $I(t) = I_0 + S_0 - S(t) + \rho \ln\frac{S(t)}{S_0}$. The logarithm carries a plus sign with argument $S(t)/S_0$, and the linear part is $S_0 - S(t)$. The distractors flip a sign or invert the logarithm argument.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: As $t \to \infty$ in an SIR epidemic, the limiting behaviour of the infective and susceptible classes is
questions.explanation: At the end of an SIR epidemic the infectives die out, $I_\infty \to 0$, while a positive fraction of susceptibles is never infected, so $S_\infty > 0$. The epidemic stops not because susceptibles run out but because $S$ falls below the threshold $\rho$ and new infections can no longer keep pace with recoveries. The other limits contradict this final size behaviour.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Dividing $\dfrac{dS}{dt}$ by $\dfrac{dR}{dt}$ in the SIR model and integrating gives the relation
questions.explanation: Since $\frac{dS}{dR} = \frac{-\beta S I}{\gamma I} = -\frac{S}{\rho}$ with $\rho = \gamma/\beta$, separating variables gives $\frac{dS}{S} = -\frac{dR}{\rho}$, so $S(t) = S_0 e^{-R(t)/\rho}$. The exponent is negative and divides $R$ by $\rho$. The variants use the wrong sign, multiply by $\rho$ instead of dividing, or solve for the wrong variable.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: The Kermack and McKendrick final size relation for the SIR model is
questions.explanation: The final size relation is $\ln\frac{S_0}{S_\infty} = R_0\left(1 - \frac{S_\infty}{N}\right)$, linking the basic reproduction number to the fraction of the population that escapes infection. It uses the logarithm of $S_0/S_\infty$ (not its reciprocal and not the ratio itself) and the factor $1 - S_\infty/N$. The distractors invert the logarithm argument, drop the logarithm, or omit the $1$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: In an SIR epidemic the proportion of susceptibles who eventually catch the disease is
questions.explanation: The fraction of the initial susceptibles that are eventually infected is $P = 1 - \frac{S_\infty}{S_0}$, since $S_\infty$ are those never infected and $S_0 - S_\infty$ catch the disease. Taking just $S_\infty/S_0$ gives the escaping fraction, and inverting the ratio or using $N$ in the numerator does not represent the attack proportion.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: An SIR disease has $\beta = 0.002$ per day and $\gamma = 0.5$ per day. The relative removal rate $\rho = \gamma/\beta$ is
questions.explanation: Compute $\rho = \gamma/\beta = 0.5 / 0.002 = 250$. So the infective curve peaks when $S$ falls to $250$, and the epidemic grows only while $S > 250$. The value $0.004$ inverts the ratio, and the others come from arithmetic slips. The correct relative removal rate is $250$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: In an SIR model with $\beta = 0.001$ per day and $\gamma = 0.4$ per day, the peak of the infective curve occurs when the number of susceptibles is
questions.explanation: The peak occurs at $S = \gamma/\beta = 0.4 / 0.001 = 400$. At that susceptible level $\frac{dI}{dt} = 0$ and the infectives turn from rising to falling. The value $2500$ inverts the ratio. The peak susceptible count is $400$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: In an SIR outbreak the population is $N = 5000$, and at the end $S_\infty = 2000$ susceptibles were never infected. What proportion of the susceptibles caught the disease, taking $S_0 \approx N$?
questions.explanation: With $S_0 \approx N = 5000$, the proportion infected is $P = 1 - \frac{S_\infty}{S_0} = 1 - \frac{2000}{5000} = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$. So $60\%$ of the susceptibles were infected and $40\%$ escaped. Reporting $0.4$ gives the escaping fraction instead. The attack proportion is $0.6$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: An influenza outbreak spreads through $N = 20000$ people, and at the end $40\%$ of them are still susceptible. Taking $S_0 \approx N$, how many individuals contracted the virus?
questions.explanation: If $40\%$ remain susceptible, then $S_\infty = 0.40 \times 20000 = 8000$ were never infected, so the number infected is $20000 - 8000 = 12000$. Equivalently $60\%$ caught the disease. Reporting $8000$ gives those who escaped, not those infected. The number who contracted the virus is $12000$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Which quantity does the final size relation of an SIR epidemic let you determine?
options: The exact time at which the infective curve peaks
The latent period of the disease
The total number of people who eventually contract the disease
questions.explanation: The final size relation connects $R_0$, $S_0$, and $S_\infty$, so once it is solved it gives the total number of people who eventually catch the disease over the whole outbreak. It does not pin down the timing of the peak, recover the rate $\beta$, or describe a latent period, all of which require other parts of the model. Its purpose is the final epidemic size.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: In the SIR model, what does the equation $\dfrac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I$ express?
options: Recovered individuals accumulate at a rate proportional to the current number of infectives
Recovered individuals can become infective again
The recovered class shrinks as people lose immunity
Recovery happens at a constant rate regardless of how many are sick
questions.explanation: The term $\gamma I$ says recovered individuals are added at a rate proportional to the number currently infective, with proportionality constant $\gamma$. In the basic SIR model recovery is permanent, so $R$ never decreases and no one returns to $I$ or $S$. A constant recovery rate would not depend on $I$, but here it does. The equation is a permanent, $I$ proportional inflow into $R$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Why does an SIR epidemic eventually stop even though susceptibles still remain in the population?
options: Because every susceptible eventually becomes infected
Because the susceptible count $S$ falls below the threshold $\rho = \gamma/\beta$, so recoveries outpace new infections
Because the transmission rate $\beta$ drops to zero on its own
Because recovered people return to the susceptible class
questions.explanation: Once $S$ drops below $\rho = \gamma/\beta$, the growth factor $\beta S - \gamma$ becomes negative, so new infections can no longer replace those leaving through recovery and the epidemic declines to zero. Susceptibles are not exhausted, the parameter $\beta$ is fixed, and in the basic SIR model recovery is permanent with no return to $S$. The mechanism is the susceptible level falling through the threshold.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Using $S(t) = S_0 e^{-R(t)/\rho}$ at the end of the epidemic (where $R_\infty = N - S_\infty$), which relation holds?
questions.explanation: At $t \to \infty$ the infectives vanish, so $R_\infty = N - S_\infty$. Substituting into $S(t) = S_0 e^{-R(t)/\rho}$ gives $S_\infty = S_0 e^{-(N - S_\infty)/\rho}$, that is $\frac{S_\infty}{S_0} = e^{-(N - S_\infty)/\rho}$. The exponent is negative and uses $N - S_\infty$, the total removed by the end. The distractors flip the sign, drop $S_\infty$ from the exponent, or turn the relation into a sum.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: In an SIR model with $\beta = 0.003$ and $\gamma = 0.6$, the infective curve is still rising when $S = 250$. Is the epidemic growing or declining at that moment?
questions.explanation: Here $\rho = \gamma/\beta = 0.6/0.003 = 200$. Since $S = 250 > 200 = \rho$, the factor $\beta S - \gamma$ is positive, so $\frac{dI}{dt} > 0$ and the epidemic is growing. It would peak at $S = 200$ and decline for $S < 200$. With $S$ above the threshold, infectives are increasing.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: At the very start of an SIR epidemic, why is the approximation $S_0 + I_0 \approx N$ used?
options: Because $R(0) = 0$, so $S_0 + I_0 + R(0) = N$ reduces to $S_0 + I_0 = N$
Because the population is not yet constant
questions.explanation: The conservation law $S(0) + I(0) + R(0) = N$ with $R(0) = 0$ gives $S_0 + I_0 = N$ exactly, and since the initial infective seed $I_0$ is small, $S_0 \approx N$. This is why $R_0 = \beta S_0/\gamma$ is often written $R_0 = \beta N/\gamma$ at onset. The other options misstate the initial values or the constancy of the population.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: In the orbit relation $I(t) = I_0 + S_0 - S(t) + \rho \ln\dfrac{S(t)}{S_0}$, what does the logarithmic term capture?
options: The recoveries accumulated as susceptibles are converted to infectives
The latent period of exposed individuals
The loss of immunity in recovered individuals
questions.explanation: The term $\rho \ln\frac{S(t)}{S_0}$ arises from integrating the recovery contribution along the orbit, accounting for the removals that occur while susceptibles are being infected. Because $S(t) < S_0$ the logarithm is negative, reducing $I$ below the simple difference $S_0 - S(t)$. There is no birth, latent, or immunity loss mechanism in this model without vital dynamics, so the term reflects recoveries.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Work out the threshold susceptible level and growth status for an SIR epidemic.
questions.solution: An SIR disease has $\beta = 0.004$ per day and $\gamma = 0.8$ per day. The relative removal rate is $\rho = \gamma/\beta =$ {{1}}. The infective curve peaks when the number of susceptibles equals this value. If at some moment $S = 300$, then since $S$ is {{2}} the threshold, the epidemic is {{3}} at that moment.
questions.explanation: The relative removal rate is $\rho = \gamma/\beta = 0.8/0.004 = 200$, which is the peak susceptible level. Since $S = 300 > 200$, the factor $\beta S - \gamma$ is positive, so $\frac{dI}{dt} > 0$ and the epidemic is still growing.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Find the final epidemic size from the remaining susceptibles.
questions.solution: An SIR epidemic runs through a town of $N = 8000$. At the end, $S_\infty = 2000$ people were never infected. Taking $S_0 \approx N$, the number who contracted the disease is {{1}}. The proportion of susceptibles who caught it is $P = 1 - S_\infty/S_0 =$ {{2}}. The infective population at the end satisfies $I_\infty =$ {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Those infected number $N - S_\infty = 8000 - 2000 = 6000$. The proportion infected is $P = 1 - 2000/8000 = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$. At the end of an SIR epidemic the infectives die out, so $I_\infty = 0$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Assemble the SIR equations and their key derived relations.
questions.solution: In the SIR model without vital dynamics, $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -\beta S I$, and the infective equation is $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I -$ {{1}}. Dividing the infective equation by the susceptible equation gives $\dfrac{dI}{dS} = -1 +$ {{2}}, where $\rho = \gamma/\beta$. At the end of the epidemic the number of infectives tends to {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The infective equation removes recoveries at rate $\gamma I$, giving $\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I - \gamma I$. Dividing by $\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta S I$ yields $\frac{dI}{dS} = -1 + \frac{\gamma}{\beta S} = -1 + \frac{\rho}{S}$. As $t \to \infty$ the infectives vanish, so $I_\infty = 0$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: State and interpret the final size relation.
questions.solution: The Kermack and McKendrick final size relation is $\ln\dfrac{S_0}{S_\infty} = R_0\left(1 - \dfrac{S_\infty}{N}\right)$. The quantity $N - S_\infty$ is the total number of people who {{1}} during the outbreak. This relation is most useful for finding the {{2}} of the epidemic. The peak of the infective curve, by contrast, occurs when $S$ falls to $\gamma/\beta$, which equals {{3}} for $\beta = 0.01$, $\gamma = 5$.
questions.explanation: In the final size relation $N - S_\infty$ counts everyone who caught the disease over the whole outbreak, so the relation gives the final epidemic size. The peak susceptible level is $\gamma/\beta = 5/0.01 = 500$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Decide the direction of an SIR epidemic from the susceptible level.
questions.solution: For an SIR disease with $\beta = 0.002$ and $\gamma = 0.5$, the threshold susceptible level is $\rho =$ {{1}}. The infectives grow while $S > \rho$ and decline while $S < \rho$. If currently $S = 200$, the epidemic is {{2}}. The largest possible value the infective curve can reach is attained exactly when $S$ equals $\rho$, where $\dfrac{dI}{dt}$ equals {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The threshold is $\rho = \gamma/\beta = 0.5/0.002 = 250$. Since $S = 200 < 250$, the growth factor $\beta S - \gamma$ is negative, so the epidemic is declining. At the peak $S = \rho$, the rate $\frac{dI}{dt} = (\beta S - \gamma)I = 0$.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology

questions.stem: Relate the relative removal rate to the basic reproduction number at onset.
questions.solution: In the SIR model the relative removal rate is $\rho = \gamma/\beta$, and at the start of an epidemic with $S_0 \approx N$ the basic reproduction number is $R_0 = \beta N/\gamma$. Therefore $R_0$ and $\rho$ are related by $R_0 = N /$ {{1}}. If $N = 1200$ and $\rho = 600$, then $R_0 =$ {{2}}, so an epidemic {{3}} occur.
questions.explanation: Since $R_0 = \beta N/\gamma$ and $\rho = \gamma/\beta$, we have $R_0 = N/\rho$. With $N = 1200$ and $\rho = 600$, $R_0 = 1200/600 = 2 > 1$, so an epidemic will occur.
questions.source: in the style of Hethcote / Brauer and Castillo-Chavez, Mathematical Models in Population Biology and Epidemiology
